title: A chicken first aid kit
subtitle: What to keep on hand for minor injuries and common ailments.
excerpt: A chicken that has been pecked, cut, or is showing early signs of illness needs attention before a vet visit is possible. A small kit with antiseptic spray, a wound dressing, and an isolation pen keeps options open and prevents minor injuries from escalating.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, gov.uk. BHWT (British Hen Welfare Trust) welfare guidance. RSPCA poultry care advice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A separate, secure housing unit used to keep an injured or ill bird away from the rest of the flock while it recovers.
A condition where part of the hen's oviduct protrudes through the vent. Requires immediate isolation and veterinary assessment.

content.text: Chickens hide illness well, a bird that appears slightly off in the morning may be seriously unwell by evening. Having a small kit assembled before it's needed means you respond to the injury rather than hunting for supplies while the situation worsens.
The kit
Veterinary antiseptic wound spray (purple, iodine-based). Use on pecking wounds, comb cuts, and minor abrasions.
Sterile saline solution for rinsing wounds.
Petroleum jelly or udder cream for treating early frostbite on combs and wattles in winter.
Poultry louse and mite powder for spot treatment.
Clean towels or cloths for restraining and wrapping a bird.
A broody coop or ventilated cardboard box to use as a short-term
isolation pen
Latex or nitrile gloves.
Your vet's number on a card in the kit.
Pecking wounds

Chickens peck blood. A small wound from a feather pull or minor injury that bleeds even slightly will be enlarged by the rest of the flock within hours. Isolate the injured bird immediately, rinse the wound with saline, apply antiseptic spray, and assess the depth.

A surface wound a centimetre or less, clean and not deep, can usually be managed without veterinary help. A wound that exposes muscle or involves the neck or vent, or that continues to bleed after ten minutes of gentle pressure, needs veterinary assessment.
Vent prolapse
A
vent prolapse

is an emergency. Isolate the bird immediately, away from the flock, in a dark quiet space. Apply cold water or petroleum jelly to the exposed tissue to keep it from drying. Phone your vet. Do not delay, the prolapsed tissue will be pecked by other birds within minutes if the isolation is incomplete.
Lethargy and non-specific illness

A bird that is standing hunched, eyes half-closed, separated from the flock, or not eating needs to be isolated and assessed. Check the crop (should be full in the evening, empty in the morning). Check the vent for signs of blockage or diarrhoea. Check the keel bone, a thin keel that was previously covered indicates weight loss.
If the bird has eaten nothing and is deteriorating over twenty-four hours in isolation, a vet visit for a hands-on assessment is appropriate. Chickens decline quickly once they stop eating.

Notifiable disease in poultry includes Newcastle disease and avian influenza. If multiple birds die within a short period, or if you see neurological signs (twisted neck, circling) in several birds, contact APHA rather than your general vet. These are reportable.
